f = (y - 1) / log (y);

[Solution : the first algorithm is inaccurate due to cancellation errors, while the second one (in presence of round digit) is stable and accurate.]

15. In binary arithmetic one can show [Dek71] that the rounding error in the sum of two numbers $a$ and $b$ , with $a \geq b$ , can be computed as

$$
((a \boxed {+} b) \boxed {-} a) \boxed {-} b).
$$

Based on this property, a method has been proposed, called Kahan compensated sum, to compute the sum of $_ { n }$ addends $a _ { i }$ in such a way that the rounding errors are compensated. In practice, letting the initial rounding error $e _ { 1 } = 0$ and $s _ { 1 } ~ = ~ u _ { 1 }$ , at the $_ i$ -th step, with $i \geq 2$ , the algorithm evaluates $y _ { i } = x _ { i } - e _ { i - 1 }$ , the sum is updated setting $s _ { i } = s _ { i - 1 } + y _ { i }$ and the new rounding error is computed as $e _ { i } = ( s _ { i } - s _ { i - 1 } ) - y _ { i }$ . Implement this algorithm in MATLAB and check its accuracy by evaluating again the second expression in (2.40).

16. The area $A ( T )$ of a triangle $T$ with sides $a$ , $^ { b }$ and $c$ , can be computed using the following formula

$$
A (T) = \sqrt {p (p - a) (p - b) (p - c)},
$$

where $p$ is half the perimeter of $T$ . Show that in the case of strongly deformed triangles ( $a \simeq b + c$ ), this formula lacks accuracy and check this experimentally.

# 3

# Direct Methods for the Solution of Linear Systems

A system of $m$ linear equations in $n$ unknowns consists of a set of algebraic relations of the form

$$
\sum_ {j = 1} ^ {n} a _ {i j} x _ {j} = b _ {i}, \quad i = 1, \dots , m \tag {3.1}
$$

where $x _ { j }$ are the unknowns, $a _ { i j }$ are the coefficients of the system and $b _ { i }$ are the components of the right hand side. System (3.1) can be more conveniently written in matrix form as

$$
\mathrm {A} \mathbf {x} = \mathbf {b}, \tag {3.2}
$$

where we have denoted by $\mathrm { A } = ( a _ { i j } ) \in \mathbb { C } ^ { m \times n }$ the coefficient matrix, by b $\mathbf { \Psi } = ( b _ { i } ) \in \mathbb { C } ^ { m }$ the right side vector and by $\mathbf { x } { = } ( x _ { i } ) \ \in \ \mathbb { C } ^ { n }$ the unknown vector, respectively. We call a solution of (3.2) any $_ { n }$ -tuple of values $x _ { i }$ which satisfies (3.1).

In this chapter we shall be mainly dealing with real-valued square systems of order $n$ , that is, systems of the form (3.2) with $\mathrm { A } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n \times n }$ and $\mathbf { b } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ . In such cases existence and uniqueness of the solution of (3.2) are ensured if one of the following (equivalent) hypotheses holds:

1. A is invertible;   
2. rank(A)= $n$   
3. the homogeneous system Ax=0 admits only the null solution.

The solution of system (3.2) is formally provided by Cramer’s rule

$$
x _ {j} = \frac {\Delta_ {j}}{\operatorname* {d e t} (\mathrm {A})}, \quad j = 1, \dots , n, \tag {3.3}
$$

where $\Delta _ { j }$ is the determinant of the matrix obtained by substituting the $j$ -th column of A with the right hand side b. This formula is, however, of little practical use. Indeed, if the determinants are evaluated by the recursive relation (1.4), the computational effort of Cramer’s rule is of the order of $( n + 1 )$ ! flops and therefore turns out to be unacceptable even for small dimensions of A (for instance, a computer able to perform $1 0 ^ { 9 }$ flops per second would take $9 . 6 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 4 7 }$ years to solve a linear system of only 50 equations).

For this reason, numerical methods that are alternatives to Cramer’s rule have been developed. They are called direct methods if they yield the solution of the system in a finite number of steps, iterative if they require (theoretically) an infinite number of steps. Iterative methods will be addressed in the next chapter. We notice from now on that the choice between a direct and an iterative method does not depend only on the theoretical efficiency of the scheme, but also on the particular type of matrix, on memory storage requirements and, finally, on the architecture of the computer.

# 3.1 Stability Analysis of Linear Systems

Solving a linear system by a numerical method invariably leads to the introduction of rounding errors. Only using stable numerical methods can keep away the propagation of such errors from polluting the accuracy of the solution. In this section two aspects of stability analysis will be addressed.

Firstly, we will analyze the sensitivity of the solution of (3.2) to changes in the data A and $\mathbf { b }$ (forward a priori analysis). Secondly, assuming that an approximate solution $\widehat { \mathbf { x } }$ of (3.2) is available, we shall quantify the per-turbations on the data A and $\mathbf { b }$ in order for $\widehat { \mathbf { x } }$ to be the exact solution of a perturbed system (backward a priori analysis). The size of these perturbations will in turn allow us to measure the accuracy of the computed solution $\widehat { \mathbf { x } }$ by the use of a posteriori analysis.

# 3.1.1 The Condition Number of a Matrix

The condition number of a matrix $\mathrm { A } \in \mathbb { C } ^ { n \times n }$ is defined as

$$
K (\mathrm {A}) = \| \mathrm {A} \| \| \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \|, \tag {3.4}
$$

where $\| \cdot \|$ is an induced matrix norm. In general $K ( \mathrm { A } )$ depends on the choice of the norm; this will be made clear by introducing a subscript

into the notation, for instance, $K _ { \infty } ( \mathrm { A } ) = \| \mathrm { A } \| _ { \infty } \ \| \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \| _ { \infty } ,$ . More generally, $K _ { p } ( \mathrm { A } )$ will denote the condition number of A in the $p$ -norm. Remarkable instances are $p = 1$ , $p = 2$ and $p = \infty$ (we refer to Exercise 1 for the relations among $K _ { 1 } ( \mathrm { A } )$ , $K _ { 2 } ( \mathrm { A } )$ and $K _ { \infty } ( \mathrm { A } )$ ).

As already noticed in Example 2.3, an increase in the condition number produces a higher sensitivity of the solution of the linear system to changes in the data. Let us start by noticing that $K ( \mathrm { A } ) \geq 1$ since

$$
1 = \| \mathrm {A A} ^ {- 1} \| \leq \| \mathrm {A} \| \| \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \| = K (\mathrm {A}).
$$

Moreover, ${ \cal K } ( \mathrm { A ^ { - 1 } } ) = { \cal K } ( \mathrm { A } )$ and $\forall \alpha \in \mathbb { C }$ with $\alpha \neq 0$ , ${ \cal K } ( \alpha \mathrm { A } ) = { \cal K } ( \mathrm { A } )$ . Finally, if A is orthogonal, ${ \cal K } _ { 2 } ( \mathrm { A } ) = 1$ since $\| \mathbf { A } \| _ { 2 } = { \sqrt { \rho ( \mathbf { A } ^ { T } \mathbf { A } ) } } = { \sqrt { \rho ( \mathbf { I } ) } } = 1$ and $\mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } = \mathrm { A } ^ { T }$ . The condition number of a singular matrix is set equal to infinity.

For $p = 2$ , $K _ { 2 } ( \mathrm { A } )$ can be characterized as follows. Starting from (1.21), it can be proved that

$$
K _ {2} (\mathrm {A}) = \| \mathrm {A} \| _ {2} \| \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \| _ {2} = \frac {\sigma_ {1} (\mathrm {A})}{\sigma_ {n} (\mathrm {A})}
$$

where $\sigma _ { 1 } ( \mathrm { A } )$ and $\sigma _ { n } ( \mathrm { A } )$ are the maximum and minimum singular values of A (see Property 1.7). As a consequence, in the case of symmetric positive definite matrices we have

$$
K _ {2} (\mathrm {A}) = \frac {\lambda_ {m a x}}{\lambda_ {m i n}} = \rho (\mathrm {A}) \rho \left(\mathrm {A} ^ {- 1}\right) \tag {3.5}
$$

where $\lambda _ { m a x }$ and $\lambda _ { m i n }$ are the maximum and minimum eigenvalues of A. To check (3.5), notice that

$$
\| \mathrm {A} \| _ {2} = \sqrt {\rho (\mathrm {A} ^ {T} \mathrm {A})} = \sqrt {\rho (\mathrm {A} ^ {2})} = \sqrt {\lambda_ {m a x} ^ {2}} = \lambda_ {m a x}.
$$

Moreover, since $\lambda ( \mathrm { A ^ { - 1 } } ) = 1 / \lambda ( \mathrm { A } )$ , one gets $\| \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \| _ { 2 } = 1 / \lambda _ { m i n }$ from which (3.5) follows. For that reason, $K _ { 2 } ( \mathrm { A } )$ is called spectral condition number.

Remark 3.1 Define the relative distance of $ { \mathrm { ~ A ~ } } \in \mathbb { C } ^ { n \times n }$ from the set of singular matrices with respect to the $p$ -norm by

$$
\operatorname {d i s t} _ {p} (\mathrm {A}) = \min  \left\{\frac {\| \delta \mathrm {A} \| _ {p}}{\| \mathrm {A} \| _ {p}}: \mathrm {A} + \delta \mathrm {A} \text {i s} \text {s i n g u l a r} \right\}.
$$

It can then be shown that ([Kah66], [Gas83])

$$
\operatorname {d i s t} _ {p} (\mathrm {A}) = \frac {1}{K _ {p} (\mathrm {A})}. \tag {3.6}
$$

Equation (3.6) suggests that a matrix A with a high condition number can behave like a singular matrix of the form A+δA. In other words, null

perturbations in the right hand side do not necessarily yield non vanishing changes in the solution since, if A+δA is singular, the homogeneous system $( \mathrm { A } + \delta \mathrm { A } ) \mathbf { z } = \mathbf { 0 }$ does no longer admit only the null solution. From (3.6) it also follows that if A+δA is nonsingular then

$$
\| \delta \mathrm {A} \| _ {p} \| \mathrm {A} \| _ {p} <   1. \tag {3.7}
$$

Relation (3.6) seems to suggest that a natural candidate for measuring the ill-conditioning of a matrix is its determinant, since from (3.3) one is prompted to conclude that small determinants mean nearly-singular matrices. However this conclusion is wrong, as there exist examples of matrices with small (respectively, high) determinants and small (respectively, high) condition numbers (see Exercise 2).

# 3.1.2 Forward a priori Analysis

In this section we introduce a measure of the sensitivity of the system to changes in the data. These changes will be interpreted in Section 3.10 as being the effects of rounding errors induced by the numerical method used to solve the system. For a more comprehensive analysis of the subject we refer to [Dat95], [GL89], [Ste73] and [Var62].

Due to rounding errors, a numerical method for solving (3.2) does not provide the exact solution but only an approximate one, which satisfies a perturbed system. In other words, a numerical method yields an (exact) solution $\mathbf { x } + \delta \mathbf { x }$ of the perturbed system

$$
(\mathrm {A} + \delta \mathrm {A}) (\mathbf {x} + \delta \mathbf {x}) = \mathbf {b} + \delta \mathbf {b}. \tag {3.8}
$$

The next result provides an estimate of $\delta \mathbf { x }$ in terms of $\delta \mathrm { A }$ and $\delta \mathbf { b }$ .

Theorem 3.1 Let $\mathrm { A } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n \times n }$ be a nonsingular matrix and $\delta \mathrm { A } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n \times n }$ be such that (3.7) is satisfied for a matrix norm $\| \cdot \|$ . Then, if $\mathbf { x } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ is the solution of Ax=b with $\mathbf { b } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ ( $\mathbf b \neq \mathbf 0$ ) and $\ b { \delta \mathbf { x } } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ satisfies (3.8) for $\pmb { \delta } \mathbf { b } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ ,

$$
\frac {\| \delta \mathbf {x} \|}{\| \mathbf {x} \|} \leq \frac {K (\mathrm {A})}{1 - K (\mathrm {A}) \| \delta \mathrm {A} \| / \| \mathrm {A} \|} \left(\frac {\| \delta \mathbf {b} \|}{\| \mathbf {b} \|} + \frac {\| \delta \mathrm {A} \|}{\| \mathrm {A} \|}\right). \tag {3.9}
$$

Proof. From (3.7) it follows that the matrix A $\mathrm { - } 1 \delta \mathrm { A }$ has norm less than 1. Then, due to Theorem 1.5, $\mathrm { I } + \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \delta \mathrm { A }$ is invertible and from (1.26) it follows that

$$
\left\| \left(\mathrm {I} + \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \delta \mathrm {A}\right) ^ {- 1} \right\| \leq \frac {1}{1 - \left\| \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \delta \mathrm {A} \right\|} \leq \frac {1}{1 - \left\| \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \right\| \left\| \delta \mathrm {A} \right\|}. \tag {3.10}
$$

On the other hand, solving for $\delta \mathbf { x }$ in (3.8) and recalling that $\operatorname { A } \mathbf { x } = \mathbf { b }$ , one gets

$$
\delta \mathbf {x} = (\mathrm {I} + \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \delta \mathrm {A}) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} (\delta \mathbf {b} - \delta \mathrm {A x}),
$$

from which, passing to the norms and using (3.10), it follows that

$$
\| \delta \mathbf {x} \| \leq \frac {\| A ^ {- 1} \|}{1 - \| A ^ {- 1} \| \| \delta A \|} \left(\| \delta \mathbf {b} \| + \| \delta A \| \| \mathbf {x} \|\right).
$$

Finally, dividing both sides by $\| \mathbf { x } \|$ (which is nonzero since $\mathrm { ~ \bf ~ b ~ } \ne \mathrm { ~ \bf ~ 0 ~ }$ and A is nonsingular) and noticing that $\| \mathbf { x } \| \geq \| \mathbf { b } \| / \| \mathrm { A } \|$ , the result follows. ✸

Well-conditioning alone is not enough to yield an accurate solution of the linear system. It is indeed crucial, as pointed out in Chapter 2, to resort to stable algorithms. Conversely, ill-conditioning does not necessarily exclude that for particular choices of the right side b the overall conditioning of the system is good (see Exercise 4).

A particular case of Theorem 3.1 is the following.

Theorem 3.2 Assume that the conditions of Theorem 3.1 hold and let $\delta \mathrm { A } = 0$ . Then

$$
\frac {1}{K (\mathrm {A})} \frac {\| \delta \mathbf {b} \|}{\| \mathbf {b} \|} \leq \frac {\| \delta \mathbf {x} \|}{\| \mathbf {x} \|} \leq K (\mathrm {A}) \frac {\| \delta \mathbf {b} \|}{\| \mathbf {b} \|}. \tag {3.11}
$$

Proof. We will prove only the first inequality since the second one directly follows from (3.9). Relation $\delta \mathbf { x } = \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \delta \mathbf { b }$ yields $\| \delta \mathbf { b } \| \leq \| \mathrm { A } \|$ $\| \delta \mathbf { x } \|$ . Multiplying both sides by $\| \mathbf { x } \|$ and recalling that $\| \mathbf { x } \| \leq \| \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \| \mathbf { \tau } \| \mathbf { b } \|$ it follows that $\| \mathbf { x } \| \ \| \delta \mathbf { b } \| \leq$ $K ( \operatorname { A } ) \| \mathbf { b } \| \ \| \delta \mathbf { x } \|$ $\| \delta \mathbf { x } \|$ , which is the desired inequality. ✸

In order to employ the inequalities (3.10) and (3.11) in the analysis of propagation of rounding errors in the case of direct methods, $\| \delta \mathrm { A } \|$ and $\| \delta \mathbf { b } \|$ should be bounded in terms of the dimension of the system and of the characteristics of the floating-point arithmetic that is being used.

It is indeed reasonable to expect that the perturbations induced by a method for solving a linear system are such that $\| \delta \mathrm { A } \| \leq \gamma \| \mathrm { A } \|$ and $\left\| \delta \mathbf { b } \right\| \leq$ $\gamma \lVert \mathbf { b } \rVert$ , $\gamma$ being a positive number that depends on the roundoff unit $\mathbf { u }$ (for example, we shall assume henceforth that $\gamma = \beta ^ { 1 - t }$ , where $\beta$ is the base and $t$ is the number of digits of the mantissa of the floating-point system $\mathbb { F }$ ). In such a case (3.9) can be completed by the following theorem.

Theorem 3.3 Assume that $\| \delta \mathrm { A } \| \leq \gamma \| \mathrm { A } \|$ , $\| \delta \mathbf { b } \| \leq \gamma \| \mathbf { b } \|$ with $\gamma \in \mathbb { R } ^ { + }$ and $\delta \mathrm { A } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n \times n }$ , $\delta \mathbf { b } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ . Then, if $\gamma K ( \mathrm { A } ) < 1$ the following inequalities hold

$$
\frac {\| \mathbf {x} + \delta \mathbf {x} \|}{\| \mathbf {x} \|} \leq \frac {1 + \gamma K (\mathrm {A})}{1 - \gamma K (\mathrm {A})}, \tag {3.12}
$$

$$
\frac {\| \boldsymbol {\delta} \mathbf {x} \|}{\| \mathbf {x} \|} \leq \frac {2 \gamma}{1 - \gamma K (\mathrm {A})} K (\mathrm {A}). \tag {3.13}
$$

Proof. From (3.8) it follows that $( \mathrm { I } + \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \delta \mathrm { A } ) ( \mathbf { x } + \delta \mathbf { x } ) = \mathbf { x } + \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \delta \mathbf { b }$ . Moreover, since $\gamma K ( \mathrm { A } ) < 1$ and $\| \delta \mathrm { A } \| \leq \gamma \| \mathrm { A } \|$ it turns out that $\mathrm { I } + \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \delta \mathrm { A }$ is nonsingular. Taking the inverse of such a matrix and passing to the norms we get $\| \mathbf { x } + \delta \mathbf { x } \| \leq$ $\lVert ( \mathrm { I } + \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \delta \mathrm { A } ) ^ { - 1 } \rVert \left( \lVert \mathbf { x } \rVert + \gamma \lVert \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \rVert \mathbf { \Omega } \rVert \right)$ . From Theorem 1.5 it then follows that

$$
\| \mathbf {x} + \delta \mathbf {x} \| \leq \frac {1}{1 - \| \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \delta \mathrm {A} \|} \left(\| \mathbf {x} \| + \gamma \| \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \| \| \mathbf {b} \|\right),
$$

which implies (3.12), since $\| \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \delta \mathrm { A } \| \leq \gamma K ( \mathrm { A } )$ and $\| \mathbf { b } \| \leq \| \mathbf { A } \| \ \| \mathbf { x } \|$ .

Let us prove (3.13). Subtracting (3.2) from (3.8) it follows that

$$
\mathrm {A} \delta \mathbf {x} = - \delta \mathrm {A} (\mathbf {x} + \delta \mathbf {x}) + \delta \mathbf {b}.
$$

Inverting A and passing to the norms, the following inequality is obtained

$$
\begin{array}{l} \| \delta \mathbf {x} \| \leq \| A ^ {- 1} \delta A \| \| \mathbf {x} + \delta \mathbf {x} \| + \| A ^ {- 1} \| \| \delta \mathbf {b} \| \tag {3.14} \\ \leq \gamma K (A) \| x + \delta x \| + \gamma \| A ^ {- 1} \| \| b \|. \\ \end{array}
$$

Dividing both sides by $\| \mathbf { x } \|$ and using the triangular inequality $\| \mathbf { x } + \pmb { \delta } \mathbf { x } \| \leq \| \pmb { \delta } \mathbf { x } \| +$ $\| \mathbf { x } \|$ , we finally get (3.13). ✸

Remarkable instances of perturbations $\delta \mathrm { A }$ and $\delta { \bf b }$ are those for which $| \delta \mathrm { A } | \leq \gamma | \mathrm { A } |$ and $| \delta \mathbf { b } | \leq \gamma | \mathbf { b } |$ with $\gamma \geq 0$ . Hereafter, the absolute value notation $\mathrm { B } = | \mathrm { A } |$ denotes the matrix $n \times n$ having entries $b _ { i j } = | a _ { i j } |$ with $i , j ~ = ~ 1 , \ldots , n$ and the inequality C  D, with $\mathrm { C } , \mathrm { D } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { m \times n }$ has the following meaning

$$
c _ {i j} \leq d _ {i j} \text {f o r} i = 1, \dots , m, j = 1, \dots , n.
$$

If $\| \cdot \| _ { \infty }$ is considered, from (3.14) it follows that

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\| \delta \mathbf {x} \| _ {\infty}}{\| \mathbf {x} \| _ {\infty}} \leq \gamma \frac {\| | \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} | | \mathrm {A} | | \mathbf {x} | + | \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} | | \mathbf {b} | \| _ {\infty}}{(1 - \gamma \| | \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} | | \mathrm {A} | \| _ {\infty}) \| \mathbf {x} \| _ {\infty}} \tag {3.15} \\ \leq \frac {2 \gamma}{1 - \gamma \| | A ^ {- 1} | | A | \| _ {\infty}} \| | A ^ {- 1} | | A | \| _ {\infty}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Estimate (3.15) is generally too pessimistic; however, the following componentwise error estimates of $\delta \mathbf { x }$ can be derived from (3.15)

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left| \delta x _ {i} \right| \leq \gamma \left| \mathbf {r} _ {(i)} ^ {T} \right| | \mathrm {A} | | \mathbf {x} + \delta \mathbf {x} |, \quad i = 1, \dots , n \quad \text {i f} \delta \mathbf {b} = \mathbf {0}, \\ \frac {\left| \delta x _ {i} \right|}{\left| x _ {i} \right|} \leq \gamma \frac {\left| \mathbf {r} _ {(i)} ^ {T} \right| | \mathbf {b} |}{\left| \mathbf {r} _ {(i)} ^ {T} \mathbf {b} \right|}, \quad i = 1, \dots , n \quad \text {i f} \delta A = 0, \tag {3.16} \\ \end{array}
$$

being $\mathbf { r } _ { ( i ) } ^ { T }$ the row vector ${ \bf e } _ { i } ^ { { \scriptscriptstyle T } } { \mathrm { A } } ^ { - 1 }$ . Estimates (3.16) are more stringent than (3.15), as can be seen in Example 3.1. The first inequality in (3.16) can be used when the perturbed solution $\mathbf { x } + \delta \mathbf { x }$ is known, being henceforth $\mathbf { x } + \delta \mathbf { x }$ the solution computed by a numerical method.

In the case where $\left| \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \right| \ \left| \mathbf { b } \right| = \left| \mathbf { x } \right|$ , the parameter $\gamma$ in (3.15) is equal to 1. For such systems the components of the solution are insensitive to perturbations to the right side. A slightly worse situation occurs when A is a triangular M-matrix and $\mathbf { b }$ has positive entries. In such a case $\gamma$ is bounded by $2 n - 1$ , since

$$
| \mathbf {r} _ {(i)} ^ {T} | | \mathrm {A} | | \mathbf {x} | \leq (2 n - 1) | x _ {i} |.
$$

For further details on the subject we refer to [Ske79], [CI95] and [Hig89]. Results linking componentwise estimates to normwise estimates through the so-called hypernorms can be found in [ADR92].

Example 3.1 Consider the linear system Ax=b with

$$
\mathbf {A} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \alpha & \frac {1}{\alpha} \\ 0 & \frac {1}{\alpha} \end{array} \right], \quad \mathbf {b} = \left[ \begin{array}{c} \alpha^ {2} + \frac {1}{\alpha} \\ \frac {1}{\alpha} \end{array} \right]
$$

which has solution $\mathbf { x } ^ { T } = \left( \alpha , 1 \right)$ , where $0 \textless \alpha \textless 1$ . Let us compare the results obtained using (3.15) and (3.16). From

$$
\left| \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \right| | \mathrm {A} | | \mathbf {x} | = \left| \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \right| | \mathbf {b} | = \left(\alpha + \frac {2}{\alpha^ {2}}, 1\right) ^ {T} \tag {3.17}
$$

it follows that the supremum of (3.17) is unbounded as $\alpha  0$ , exactly as it happens in the case of $\| \mathrm { A } \| _ { \infty }$ . On the other hand, the amplification factor of the error in (3.16) is bounded. Indeed, the component of the maximum absolute value, $x _ { 2 }$ , of the solution, satisfies $| \mathbf { r } _ { ( 2 ) } ^ { T } |$ A $| \mathbf { x } | / | x _ { 2 } | = 1$ . .

# 3.1.3 Backward a priori Analysis

The numerical methods that we have considered thus far do not require the explicit computation of the inverse of A to solve Ax=b. However, we can always assume that they yield an approximate solution of the form $\widehat { \mathbf { x } } = \mathrm { C b }$ , where the matrix C, due to rounding errors, is an approximation of $\mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 }$ . In practice, C is very seldom constructed; in case this should happen, the following result yields an estimate of the error that is made substituting C for A $^ { - 1 }$ (see [IK66], Chapter 2, Theorem 7).

Property 3.1 Let $\mathrm { R } = \mathrm { A C } - \mathrm { I }$ ; if $\| \mathrm { R } \| < 1$ , then A and C are nonsingular and

$$
\left\| \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \right\| \leq \frac {\left\| \mathrm {C} \right\|}{1 - \left\| \mathrm {R} \right\|}, \quad \frac {\left\| \mathrm {R} \right\|}{\left\| \mathrm {A} \right\|} \leq \left\| \mathrm {C} - \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \right\| \leq \frac {\left\| \mathrm {C} \right\| \left\| \mathrm {R} \right\|}{1 - \left\| \mathrm {R} \right\|}. \tag {3.18}
$$

In the frame of backward a priori analysis we can interpret C as being the inverse of $\mathrm { A } + \delta \mathrm { A }$ (for a suitable unknown $\delta$ A). We are thus assuming that $\mathrm { C } ( \mathrm { A } + \delta \mathrm { A } ) = \mathrm { I }$ . This yields

$$
\delta \mathrm {A} = \mathrm {C} ^ {- 1} - \mathrm {A} = - (\mathrm {A C} - \mathrm {I}) \mathrm {C} ^ {- 1} = - \mathrm {R C} ^ {- 1}
$$

and, as a consequence, if $\| \mathrm { R } \| < 1$ it turns out that

$$
\| \delta \mathrm {A} \| \leq \frac {\| \mathrm {R} \| \| \mathrm {A} \|}{1 - \| \mathrm {R} \|}, \tag {3.19}
$$

having used the first inequality in (3.18), where A is assumed to be an approximation of the inverse of C (notice that the roles of C and A can be interchanged).

# 3.1.4 A posteriori Analysis

Having approximated the inverse of A by a matrix C turns into having an approximation of the solution of the linear system (3.2). Let us denote by y a known approximate solution. The aim of the a posteriori analysis is to relate the (unknown) error $\mathbf { e } = \mathbf { y } - \mathbf { x }$ to quantities that can be computed using y and C.

The starting point of the analysis relies on the fact that the residual vector $\mathbf { r } = \mathbf { b } - \mathrm { A } \mathbf { y }$ is in general nonzero, since y is just an approximation to the unknown exact solution. The residual can be related to the error through Property 3.1 as follows. We have $\mathbf { e } = \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } ( \mathrm { A } \mathbf { y } - \mathbf { b } ) = - \mathrm { A } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { r }$ and thus, if $\| \mathrm { R } \| < 1$ then

$$
\| \mathbf {e} \| \leq \frac {\| \mathbf {r} \| \| C \|}{1 - \| R \|}. \tag {3.20}
$$

Notice that the estimate does not necessarily require y to coincide with the solution $\widehat { \mathbf { x } } = \mathrm { C b }$ of the backward a priori analysis. One could therefore think of computing C only for the purpose of using the estimate (3.20) (for instance, in the case where (3.2) is solved through the Gauss elimination method, one can compute C a posteriori using the LU factorization of A, see Sections 3.3 and 3.3.1).

We conclude by noticing that if $\scriptstyle \delta \mathbf { b }$ is interpreted in (3.11) as being the residual of the computed solution $\mathbf { y } = \mathbf { x } + \delta \mathbf { x }$ , it also follows that

$$
\frac {\| \mathbf {e} \|}{\| \mathbf {x} \|} \leq K (\mathrm {A}) \frac {\| \mathbf {r} \|}{\| \mathbf {b} \|}. \tag {3.21}
$$

The estimate (3.21) is not used in practice since the computed residual is affected by rounding errors. A more significant estimate (in the $\| \cdot \| _ { \infty }$ norm) is obtained letting ${ \widehat { \mathbf { r } } } = f l ( \mathbf { b } - \mathrm { A } \mathbf { y } )$ and assuming that $\widehat { \mathbf { r } } = \mathbf { r } + \delta \mathbf { r }$ with $\left| \delta \mathbf { r } \right| \leq \gamma _ { n + 1 } ( \left| \mathrm { A } \right| \left| \mathbf { y } \right| + \left| \mathbf { b } \right| )$ , where $\gamma _ { n + 1 } = ( n + 1 ) \mathsf { u } / ( 1 - ( n + 1 ) \mathsf { u } ) > 0$ , from which we have

$$
\frac {\| \mathbf {e} \| _ {\infty}}{\| \mathbf {y} \| _ {\infty}} \leq \frac {\| | \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} | (| \widehat {\mathbf {r}} | + \gamma_ {n + 1} (| \mathrm {A} | | \mathbf {y} | + | \mathbf {b} |)) \| _ {\infty}}{\| \mathbf {y} \| _ {\infty}}.
$$

Formulae like this last one are implemented in the library for linear algebra LAPACK (see [ABB+92]).

# 3.2 Solution of Triangular Systems

Consider the nonsingular 3 $\times$ 3 lower triangular system

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{l l l} l _ {1 1} & 0 & 0 \\ l _ {2 1} & l _ {2 2} & 0 \\ l _ {3 1} & l _ {3 2} & l _ {3 3} \end{array} \right] \quad \left[ \begin{array}{l} x _ {1} \\ x _ {2} \\ x _ {3} \end{array} \right] = \quad \left[ \begin{array}{l} b _ {1} \\ b _ {2} \\ b _ {3} \end{array} \right].
$$

Since the matrix is nonsingular, its diagonal entries $l _ { i i } , ~ i ~ = ~ 1 , 2 , 3$ $l _ { i i }$ , are non vanishing, hence we can solve sequentially for the unknown values $x _ { i } , \ i = 1 , 2 , 3$ as follows

$$
\begin{array}{l} x _ {1} = b _ {1} / l _ {1 1}, \\ x _ {2} = \left(b _ {2} - l _ {2 1} x _ {1}\right) / l _ {2 2}, \\ x _ {3} = \left(b _ {3} - l _ {3 1} x _ {1} - l _ {3 2} x _ {2}\right) / l _ {3 3}. \\ \end{array}
$$

This algorithm can be extended to systems $n \times n$ and is called forward substitution. In the case of a system Lx=b, with L being a nonsingular lower triangular matrix of order $n$ ( $n \geq 2$ ), the method takes the form

$$
\begin{array}{l} x _ {1} = \frac {b _ {1}}{l _ {1 1}}, \\ x _ {i} = \frac {1}{l _ {i i}} \left(b _ {i} - \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {i - 1} l _ {i j} x _ {j}\right), \quad i = 2, \dots , n. \tag {3.22} \\ \end{array}
$$

The number of multiplications and divisions to execute the algorithm is equal to $n ( n { + } 1 ) / 2$ , while the number of sums and subtractions is $n ( n { - } 1 ) / 2$ . The global operation count for (3.22) is thus $n ^ { 2 }$ flops.

Similar conclusions can be drawn for a linear system Ux=b, where U is a nonsingular upper triangular matrix of order $n$ ( $n \geq 2$ ). In this case the algorithm is called backward substitution and in the general case can be written as

$$
\begin{array}{l} x _ {n} = \frac {b _ {n}}{u _ {n n}}, \\ x _ {i} = \frac {1}{u _ {i i}} \left(b _ {i} - \sum_ {j = i + 1} ^ {n} u _ {i j} x _ {j}\right), \quad i = n - 1, \dots , 1. \tag {3.23} \\ \end{array}
$$

Its computational cost is still $n ^ { 2 }$ flops.

# 3.2.1 Implementation of Substitution Methods

Each $i$ -th step of algorithm (3.22) requires performing the scalar product between the row vector $\operatorname { L } ( i , 1 : i - 1 )$ (this notation denoting the vector extracted from matrix L taking the elements of the $i$ -th row from the first

to the (i-1)-th column) and the column vector $\mathbf { x } ( 1 : i - 1 )$ . The access to matrix L is thus by row; for that reason, the forward substitution algorithm, when implemented in the form above, is called row-oriented.

Its coding is reported in Program 1 (the Program mat square that is called by forward row merely checks that L is a square matrix).

Program 1 - forward row : Forward substitution: row-oriented version

function $[\mathbf{x}] =$ forward_row(L,b) $[\mathsf{n}] =$ mat_square(L); $x(1) = b(1) / L(1,1)$ for $\mathrm{i} = 2:\mathrm{n}$ $\times (\mathrm{i}) = (\mathtt{b}(\mathrm{i}) - \mathtt{L}(\mathrm{i},1:\mathrm{i} - 1)^{*}(\mathtt{x}(\mathtt{1}:\mathtt{i} - 1))') / \mathtt{L}(\mathtt{i},\mathtt{i})$ end $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}'$

To obtain a column-oriented version of the same algorithm, we take advantage of the fact that $i$ -th component of the vector $\mathbf { x }$ , once computed, can be conveniently eliminated from the system.

An implementation of such a procedure, where the solution $\mathbf { x }$ is overwritten on the right vector b, is reported in Program 2.

Program 2 - forward col : Forward substitution: column-oriented version

function $[\mathsf{b}] =$ forward_col(L,b) $[n] =$ mat_square(L);   
for $j = 1:n - 1$ $\begin{array}{rl} & {\mathsf{b}(j) = \mathsf{b}(j) / \mathsf{L}(j,j);\mathsf{b}(j + 1:n) = \mathsf{b}(j + 1:n) - \mathsf{b}(j)^{*}\mathsf{L}(j + 1:n,j);}\\ & {\mathsf{end};\mathsf{b}(n) = \mathsf{b}(n) / \mathsf{L}(n,n);} \end{array}$

Implementing the same algorithm by a row-oriented rather than a columnoriented approach, might dramatically change its performance (but of course, not the solution). The choice of the form of implementation must therefore be subordinated to the specific hardware that is used.

Similar considerations hold for the backward substitution method, presented in (3.23) in its row-oriented version.

In Program 3 only the column-oriented version of the algorithm is coded. As usual, the vector x is overwritten on b.

Program 3 - backward col : Backward substitution: column-oriented version

function $[\mathsf{b}] =$ backward_col(U,b) $[\mathsf{n}] =$ mat_square(U);   
for $\mathrm{j} = \mathrm{n}: - 1:2$ $\begin{array}{rl} & {\mathsf{b(j)} = \mathsf{b(j)} / \mathsf{U(j,j)};\mathsf{b(1:j - 1)} = \mathsf{b(1:j - 1) - b(j)*U(1:j - 1,j)}}\\ & {\mathsf{end};\mathsf{b(1)} = \mathsf{b(1) / U(1,1)}} \end{array}$

When large triangular systems must be solved, only the triangular portion of the matrix should be stored leading to considerable saving of memory resources.

# 3.2.2 Rounding Error Analysis

The analysis developed so far has not accounted for the presence of rounding errors. When including these, the forward and backward substitution algorithms no longer yield the exact solutions to the systems Lx=b and Uy=b, but rather provide approximate solutions $\widehat { \mathbf { x } }$ that can be regarded as being exact solutions to the perturbed systems

$$
(\mathrm {L} + \delta \mathrm {L}) \widehat {\mathbf {x}} = \mathbf {b}, \quad (\mathrm {U} + \delta \mathrm {U}) \widehat {\mathbf {x}} = \mathbf {b},
$$

where $\delta \mathrm { { L } } ~ = ~ ( \delta l _ { i j } )$ and $ { \delta } \mathrm { U } = ( \delta \boldsymbol { u } _ { i j } )$ are perturbation matrices. In order to apply the estimates (3.9) carried out in Section 3.1.2, we must provide estimates of the perturbation matrices, $\delta \mathrm { L }$ and $\delta$ U, as a function of the entries of L and U, of their size and of the characteristics of the floatingpoint arithmetic. For this purpose, it can be shown that

$$
| \delta \mathrm {T} | \leq \frac {n \mathrm {u}}{1 - n \mathrm {u}} | \mathrm {T} |, \tag {3.24}
$$

where T is equal to L or U, $\mathtt { u } = \mathtt { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \beta ^ { 1 - t }$ is the roundoff unit defined in (2.34). Clearly, if $n \mathtt { u } < 1$ from (3.24) it turns out that, using a Taylor expansion, $| \delta \mathrm { T } | \leq n \mathrm { u } | \mathrm { T } | + \mathcal { O } ( \mathrm { u } ^ { 2 } )$ . Moreover, from (3.24) and (3.9) it follows that, if $n \mathrm { u } K ( \mathrm { T } ) < 1$ , then

$$
\frac {\| \mathbf {x} - \widehat {\mathbf {x}} \|}{\| \mathbf {x} \|} \leq \frac {n u K (\mathrm {T})}{1 - n u K (\mathrm {T})} = n u K (\mathrm {T}) + \mathcal {O} \left(u ^ {2}\right) \tag {3.25}
$$

for the norms $\| { \cdot } \| _ { 1 } , \| { \cdot } \| _ { \infty }$ and the Frobenius norm. If $\mathbf { u }$ is sufficiently small (as typically happens), the perturbations introduced by the rounding errors in the solution of a triangular system can thus be neglected. As a consequence, the accuracy of the solution computed by the forward or backward substitution algorithm is generally very high.

These results can be improved by introducing some additional assumptions on the entries of L or U. In particular, if the entries of U are such that $| u _ { i i } | \geq | u _ { i j } |$ for any $j > i$ , then

$$
\left| x _ {i} - \widehat {x} _ {i} \right| \leq 2 ^ {n - i + 1} \frac {n \mathfrak {u}}{1 - n \mathfrak {u}} \max  _ {j \geq i} | \widehat {x} _ {j} |, \qquad 1 \leq i \leq n.
$$

The same result holds if T=L, provided that $| l _ { i i } | \geq | l _ { i j } |$ for any $j < i$ , or if L and U are diagonally dominant. The previous estimates will be employed in Sections 3.3.1 and 3.4.2.

For the proofs of the results reported so far, see [FM67], [Hig89] and [Hig88].

# 3.2.3 Inverse of a Triangular Matrix

The algorithm (3.23) can be employed to explicitly compute the inverse of an upper triangular matrix. Indeed, given an upper triangular matrix

U, the column vectors $\mathbf { v } _ { i }$ of the inverse V= $\mathbf { \Delta } _ { : } ( \mathbf { v } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathbf { v } _ { n } )$ of U satisfy the following linear systems

$$
\mathrm {U v} _ {i} = \mathbf {e} _ {i}, \quad i = 1, \dots , n \tag {3.26}
$$

where $\left\{ \mathbf { e } _ { i } \right\}$ is the canonical basis of $\mathbb { R } ^ { n }$ (defined in Example 1.3). Solving for $\mathbf { v } _ { i }$ thus requires the application of algorithm (3.23) $n$ times to (3.26).

This procedure is quite inefficient since at least half the entries of the inverse of U are null. Let us take advantage of this as follows. Denote by $\mathbf { v } _ { k } ^ { \prime } = ( v _ { 1 k } ^ { \prime } , \dots , v _ { k k } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { T }$ the vector of size $k$ such that

$$
\mathrm {U} ^ {(k)} \mathbf {v} _ {k} ^ {\prime} = \mathrm {l} _ {k} \quad k = 1, \dots , n \tag {3.27}
$$

where $\mathrm { U } ^ { ( k ) }$ is the principal submatrix of U of order $k$ and $\mathbf { l } _ { k }$ the vector of $\mathbb { R } ^ { k }$ having null entries, except the first one which is equal to 1. Systems (3.27) are upper triangular, but have order $k$ and can be again solved using the method (3.23). We end up with the following inversion algorithm for upper triangular matrices: for $k = n , n - 1 , \ldots , 1$ compute

$$
\begin{array}{l} v _ {k k} ^ {\prime} = u _ {k k} ^ {- 1}, \\ v _ {i k} ^ {\prime} = - u _ {i i} ^ {- 1} \sum_ {j = i + 1} ^ {k} u _ {i j} v _ {j k} ^ {\prime}, \quad \text {f o r} i = k - 1, k - 2, \dots , 1. \tag {3.28} \\ \end{array}
$$

At the end of this procedure the vectors $\mathbf { v } _ { k } ^ { \prime }$ furnish the non vanishing entries of the columns of U $\ l - 1$ . The algorithm requires about $n ^ { 3 } / 3 + ( 3 / 4 ) n ^ { 2 }$ flops. Once again, due to rounding errors, the algorithm (3.28) no longer yields the exact solution, but an approximation of it. The error that is introduced can be estimated using the backward a priori analysis carried out in Section 3.1.3.

A similar procedure can be constructed from (3.22) to compute the inverse of a lower triangular system.

# 3.3 The Gaussian Elimination Method (GEM) and LU Factorization

The Gaussian elimination method aims at reducing the system Ax=b to an equivalent system (that is, having the same solution) of the form Ux=b, where U is an upper triangular matrix and $\widehat { \mathbf { b } }$ is an updated right side vector. This latter system can then be solved by the backward substitution method. Let us denote the original system by $\mathbf { A } ^ { ( 1 ) } \mathbf { x } = \mathbf { b } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ . During the reduction procedure we basically employ the property which states that replacing one of the equations by the difference between this equation and another one multiplied by a non null constant yields an equivalent system (i.e., one with the same solution).

Thus, consider a nonsingular matrix $\mathrm { ~ A ~ } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n \times n }$ , and suppose that the diagonal entry $a _ { 1 1 }$ is non vanishing. Introducing the multipliers

$$
m _ {i 1} = \frac {a _ {i 1} ^ {(1)}}{a _ {1 1} ^ {(1)}}, \quad i = 2, 3, \ldots , n,
$$

where a(1)ij $a _ { i j } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ denote the elements of $\mathrm { A } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ , it is possible to eliminate the unknown $x _ { 1 }$ from the rows other than the first one by simply subtracting from row $i$ , with $i = 2 , \ldots , n$ , the first row multiplied by $m _ { i 1 }$ and doing the same on the right side. If we now define

$$
a _ {i j} ^ {(2)} = a _ {i j} ^ {(1)} - m _ {i 1} a _ {1 j} ^ {(1)}, i, j = 2, \ldots , n,
$$

$$
b _ {i} ^ {(2)} = b _ {i} ^ {(1)} - m _ {i 1} b _ {1} ^ {(1)}, \quad i = 2, \dots , n,
$$

where $b _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ denote the components of $\mathbf { b } ^ { ( 1 ) }$ , we get a new system of the form

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{c c c c} a _ {1 1} ^ {(1)} & a _ {1 2} ^ {(1)} & \ldots & a _ {1 n} ^ {(1)} \\ 0 & a _ {2 2} ^ {(2)} & \ldots & a _ {2 n} ^ {(2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\ 0 & a _ {n 2} ^ {(2)} & \ldots & a _ {n n} ^ {(2)} \end{array} \right] \quad \left[ \begin{array}{c} x _ {1} \\ x _ {2} \\ \vdots \\ x _ {n} \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{c} b _ {1} ^ {(1)} \\ b _ {2} ^ {(2)} \\ \vdots \\ b _ {n} ^ {(2)} \end{array} \right],
$$

which we denote by $\mathbf { A } ^ { ( 2 ) } \mathbf { x } = \mathbf { b } ^ { ( 2 ) }$ , that is equivalent to the starting one. Similarly, we can transform the system in such a way that the unknown $x _ { 2 }$ is eliminated from rows $3 , \ldots , n .$ . In general, we end up with the finite sequence of systems

$$
\mathrm {A} ^ {(k)} \mathbf {x} = \mathbf {b} ^ {(k)}, \quad 1 \leq k \leq n, \tag {3.29}
$$

where, for $k \geq 2$ , matrix A $( k )$ takes the following form

$$
\mathrm {A} ^ {(k)} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c c c c} a _ {1 1} ^ {(1)} & a _ {1 2} ^ {(1)} & \ldots & \ldots & \ldots & a _ {1 n} ^ {(1)} \\ 0 & a _ {2 2} ^ {(2)} & & & & a _ {2 n} ^ {(2)} \\ \vdots & & \ddots & & & \vdots \\ 0 & \ldots & 0 & a _ {k k} ^ {(k)} & \ldots & a _ {k n} ^ {(k)} \\ \vdots & & \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\ 0 & \ldots & 0 & a _ {n k} ^ {(k)} & \ldots & a _ {n n} ^ {(k)} \end{array} \right],
$$

having assumed that $a _ { i i } ^ { ( i ) } \neq 0$ for $i = 1 , \ldots , k - 1$ . It is clear that for $k = n$ we obtain the upper triangular system A(n) x = b(n) $\mathbf { A } ^ { ( n ) } \mathbf { x } = \mathbf { b } ^ { ( n ) }$

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{c c c c c} a _ {1 1} ^ {(1)} & a _ {1 2} ^ {(1)} & \ldots & \ldots & a _ {1 n} ^ {(1)} \\ 0 & a _ {2 2} ^ {(2)} & & & a _ {2 n} ^ {(2)} \\ \vdots & & \ddots & & \vdots \\ 0 & & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & & & & a _ {n n} ^ {(n)} \end{array} \right] \quad \left[ \begin{array}{c} x _ {1} \\ x _ {2} \\ \vdots \\ \vdots \\ x _ {n} \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{c} b _ {1} ^ {(1)} \\ b _ {2} ^ {(2)} \\ \vdots \\ \vdots \\ b _ {n} ^ {(n)} \end{array} \right].
$$

Consistently with the notations that have been previously introduced, we denote by U the upper triangular matrix A(n). The entries a(k)kk $\mathrm { A } ^ { ( n ) }$ $a _ { k k } ^ { ( k ) }$ are called pivots and must obviously be non null for $k = 1 , \ldots , n - 1$ .

the In order to highlight the formulae which transform the $k + 1$ -th one, for $k = 1 , \ldots , n - 1$ we assume that $a _ { k k } ^ { ( k ) } \neq 0$ $k$ -th system into and define the multiplier

$$
m _ {i k} = \frac {a _ {i k} ^ {(k)}}{a _ {k k} ^ {(k)}}, \quad i = k + 1, \dots , n. \tag {3.30}
$$

Then we let

$$
a _ {i j} ^ {(k + 1)} = a _ {i j} ^ {(k)} - m _ {i k} a _ {k j} ^ {(k)}, \quad i, j = k + 1, \dots , n
$$

$$
b _ {i} ^ {(k + 1)} = b _ {i} ^ {(k)} - m _ {i k} b _ {k} ^ {(k)}, \quad i = k + 1, \dots , n. \tag {3.31}
$$

Example 3.2 Let us use GEM to solve the following system

$$
\left(\mathrm {A} ^ {(1)} \mathbf {x} = \mathbf {b} ^ {(1)}\right) \quad \left\{ \begin{array}{r c l r c l r c l} x _ {1} & + & \frac {1}{2} x _ {2} & + & \frac {1}{3} x _ {3} & = & \frac {1 1}{6} \\ \frac {1}{2} x _ {1} & + & \frac {1}{3} x _ {2} & + & \frac {1}{4} x _ {3} & = & \frac {1 3}{1 2} \\ \frac {1}{3} x _ {1} & + & \frac {1}{4} x _ {2} & + & \frac {1}{5} x _ {3} & = & \frac {4 7}{6 0} \end{array} \right.,
$$

which admits the solution $\mathbf { x } = \left( 1 , \ 1 , \ 1 \right) ^ { T }$ . At the first step we compute the multipliers $m _ { 2 1 } = 1 / 2$ and $m _ { 3 1 } = 1 / 3$ , and subtract from the second and third equation of the system the first row multiplied by $m _ { 2 1 }$ and $m _ { 3 1 }$ , respectively. We obtain the equivalent system

$$
\left(\mathrm {A} ^ {(2)} \mathbf {x} = \mathbf {b} ^ {(2)}\right) \quad \left\{ \begin{array}{r c l r c l r c l} x _ {1} & + & \frac {1}{2} x _ {2} & + & \frac {1}{3} x _ {3} & = & \frac {1 1}{6} \\ 0 & + & \frac {1}{1 2} x _ {2} & + & \frac {1}{1 2} x _ {3} & = & \frac {1}{6} \\ 0 & + & \frac {1}{1 2} x _ {2} & + & \frac {4}{4 5} x _ {3} & = & \frac {3 1}{1 8 0} \end{array} \right..
$$

If we now subtract the second row multiplied by $m _ { 3 2 } = 1$ from the third one, we end up with the upper triangular system

$$
\left(\mathrm {A} ^ {(3)} \mathbf {x} = \mathbf {b} ^ {(3)}\right) \quad \left\{ \begin{array}{r c l r c l r c l} x _ {1} & + & \frac {1}{2} x _ {2} & + & \frac {1}{3} x _ {3} & = & \frac {1 1}{6} \\ 0 & + & \frac {1}{1 2} x _ {2} & + & \frac {1}{1 2} x _ {3} & = & \frac {1}{6} \\ 0 & + & 0 & + & \frac {1}{1 8 0} x _ {3} & = & \frac {1}{1 8 0} \end{array} \right.,
$$

from which we immediately compute $x _ { 3 } = 1$ and then, by back substitution, the remaining unknowns $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = 1$ . .

Remark 3.2 The matrix in Example 3.2 is called the Hilbert matrix of order 3. In the general $n \times n$ case, its entries are

$$
h _ {i j} = 1 / (i + j - 1), \quad i, j = 1, \dots , n. \tag {3.32}
$$

As we shall see later on, this matrix provides the paradigm of an illconditioned matrix. 

To complete Gaussian elimination $2 ( n - 1 ) n ( n + 1 ) / 3 + n ( n - 1 )$ flops are required, plus $n ^ { 2 }$ flops to backsolve the triangular system U $\mathbf { x } = \mathbf { b } ^ { ( n ) }$ . Therefore, about $( 2 n ^ { 3 } / 3 + 2 n ^ { 2 } )$ flops are needed to solve the linear system using GEM. Neglecting the lower order terms, we can state that the Gaussian elimination process has a cost of $2 n ^ { 3 } / 3$ flops.

As previously noticed, GEM terminates safely iff the pivotal elements $a _ { k k } ^ { ( k ) }$ for $k = 1 , \ldots , n - 1$ , are non vanishing. Unfortunately, having non null diagonal entries in A is not enough to prevent zero pivots to arise during the elimination process. For example, matrix A in (3.33) is nonsingular and has nonzero diagonal entries

$$
A = \left[ \begin{array}{l l l} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 5 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{array} \right], \quad A ^ {(2)} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & \boxed {0} & - 1 \\ 0 & - 6 & - 1 2 \end{array} \right]. \tag {3.33}
$$

Nevertheless, when GEM is applied, it is interrupted at the second step since a22 $a _ { 2 2 } ^ { ( 2 ) } = 0$ (2) .

More restrictive conditions on A are thus needed to ensure the applicability of the method. We shall see in Section 3.3.1 that if the leading dominating minors $d _ { i }$ of A are nonzero for $i = 1 , \ldots , n - 1$ , then the corresponding pivotal entries $a _ { i i } ^ { ( i ) }$ must necessarily be non vanishing. We recall that $d _ { i }$ is the determinant of $\mathrm { A } _ { i }$ , the $i$ -th principal submatrix made by the first $i$ rows and columns of A. The matrix in the previous example does not satisfy this condition, having $d _ { 1 } = 1$ and $d _ { 2 } = 0$ .

Classes of matrices exist such that GEM can be always safely employed in its basic form (3.31). Among them, we recall the following ones:

1. matrices diagonally dominant by rows;   
2. matrices diagonally dominant by columns. In such a case one can even show that the multipliers are in module less than or equal to 1 (see Property 3.2);   
3. matrices symmetric and positive definite (see Theorem 3.6).

For a rigorous derivation of these results, we refer to the forthcoming sections.

# 3.3.1 GEM as a Factorization Method

In this section we show how GEM is equivalent to performing a factorization of the matrix A into the product of two matrices, A=LU, with U=A $( n )$ . Since L and U depend only on A and not on the right hand side, the same factorization can be reused when solving several linear systems having the same matrix A but different right hand side b, with a considerable reduction of the operation count (indeed, the main computational effort, about $2 n ^ { 3 } / 3$ flops, is spent in the elimination procedure).

Let us go back to Example 3.2 concerning the Hilbert matrix $\mathrm { H _ { 3 } }$ . In practice, to pass from A(1)=H to the matrix A $( 2 )$ at the second step, we $^ { 3 }$ have multiplied the system by the matrix

$$
\mathrm {M} _ {1} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ - \frac {1}{2} & 1 & 0 \\ - \frac {1}{3} & 0 & 1 \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ - m _ {2 1} & 1 & 0 \\ - m _ {3 1} & 0 & 1 \end{array} \right].
$$

Indeed,

$$
\mathrm {M} _ {1} \mathrm {A} = \mathrm {M} _ {1} \mathrm {A} ^ {(1)} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} 1 & \frac {1}{2} & \frac {1}{3} \\ 0 & \frac {1}{1 2} & \frac {1}{1 2} \\ 0 & \frac {1}{1 2} & \frac {4}{4 5} \end{array} \right] = \mathrm {A} ^ {(2)}.
$$

Similarly, to perform the second (and last) step of GEM, we must multiply A $^ { ( 2 ) }$ by the matrix

$$
\mathrm {M} _ {2} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & - 1 & 1 \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & - m _ {3 2} & 1 \end{array} \right],
$$

where $\mathrm { A ^ { ( 3 ) } = M _ { 2 } A ^ { ( 2 ) } }$ . Therefore

$$
\mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {M} _ {1} \mathrm {A} = \mathrm {A} ^ {(3)} = \mathrm {U}. \tag {3.34}
$$

On the other hand, matrices M $\bot$ and M $^ 2$ are lower triangular, their product is still lower triangular, as is their inverse; thus, from (3.34) one gets

$$
\mathrm {A} = \left(\mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {M} _ {1}\right) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {U} = \mathrm {L U},
$$

which is the desired factorization of A.

This identity can be generalized as follows. Setting

$$
\mathbf {m} _ {k} = \left(0, \dots , 0, m _ {k + 1, k}, \dots , m _ {n, k}\right) ^ {T} \in \mathbb {R} ^ {n}
$$

and defining

$$
\mathrm {M} _ {k} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c c c c} 1 & \dots & 0 & 0 & \dots & 0 \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\ 0 & & 1 & 0 & & 0 \\ 0 & & - m _ {k + 1, k} & 1 & & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & \dots & - m _ {n, k} & 0 & \dots & 1 \end{array} \right] = \mathrm {I} _ {n} - \mathbf {m} _ {k} \mathbf {e} _ {k} ^ {T}
$$

as the $k$ -th Gaussian transformation matrix, one finds out that

$$
\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left. \right.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left.\left. \mathbf {M} _ {k}\right) _ {k} ^ {T}\right) _ {k} ^ {T}\right) _ {k} ^ {T}\right) _ {k} ^ {T}\right) _ {k} ^ {T}\right) _ {k} ^ {T}\right) _ {k} ^ {T}\right) _ {k} ^ {T}\right) _ {k} ^ {T}\right) _ {k} ^ {T}\right) _ {k} ^ {1}\right) _ {k} ^ {1}\right) _ {k} ^ {1}\right) _ {k} ^ {1}\right) _ {k} ^ {1}\right) _ {k} ^ {1}\right) _ {k} ^ {1}\right) _ {k} ^ {1}\right) _ {k} ^ {1}\right) _ {k} ^ {1}\bigg) _ {k} ^ {1}
$$

On the other hand, from (3.31) we have that

$$
a _ {i j} ^ {(k + 1)} = a _ {i j} ^ {(k)} - m _ {i k} \delta_ {k k} a _ {k j} ^ {(k)} = \sum_ {p = 1} ^ {n} (\delta_ {i p} - m _ {i k} \delta_ {k p}) a _ {p j} ^ {(k)}, \qquad i, j = k + 1, \ldots , n,
$$

or, equivalently,

$$
\mathrm {A} ^ {(k + 1)} = \mathrm {M} _ {k} \mathrm {A} ^ {(k)}. \tag {3.35}
$$

As a consequence, at the end of the elimination process the matrices M $k$ , with $k = 1 , \ldots , n - 1$ , and the matrix U have been generated such that

$$
\mathrm {M} _ {n - 1} \mathrm {M} _ {n - 2} \dots \mathrm {M} _ {1} \mathrm {A} = \mathrm {U}.
$$

The matrices M $k$ are unit lower triangular with inverse given by

$$
\mathrm {M} _ {k} ^ {- 1} = 2 \mathrm {I} _ {n} - \mathrm {M} _ {k} = \mathrm {I} _ {n} + \mathbf {m} _ {k} \mathbf {e} _ {k} ^ {T}, \tag {3.36}
$$

where $( \mathbf { m } _ { i } \mathbf { e } _ { i } ^ { T } ) ( \mathbf { m } _ { j } \mathbf { e } _ { j } ^ { T } )$ are equal to the null matrix if $i \neq j$ . As a consequence

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {A} = \mathrm {M} _ {1} ^ {- 1} \mathrm {M} _ {2} ^ {- 1} \dots \mathrm {M} _ {n - 1} ^ {- 1} \mathrm {U} \\ { = } { ( \mathbf { I } _ { n } + \mathbf { m } _ { 1 } \mathbf { e } _ { 1 } ^ { T } ) ( \mathbf { I } _ { n } + \mathbf { m } _ { 2 } \mathbf { e } _ { 2 } ^ { T } ) . . . ( \mathbf { I } _ { n } + \mathbf { m } _ { n - 1 } \mathbf { e } _ { n - 1 } ^ { T } ) \mathbf { U } } \\ = \left(\mathrm {I} _ {n} + \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {n - 1} \mathbf {m} _ {i} \mathbf {e} _ {i} ^ {T}\right) \mathrm {U} \\ = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c c c} 1 & 0 & \dots & \dots & 0 \\ m _ {2 1} & 1 & & & \vdots \\ \vdots & m _ {3 2} & \ddots & & \vdots \\ \vdots & \vdots & & \ddots & 0 \\ m _ {n 1} & m _ {n 2} & \dots & m _ {n, n - 1} & 1 \end{array} \right] U. \tag {3.37} \\ \end{array}
$$

Defining $\mathrm { L } = ( \mathrm { M } _ { n - 1 } \mathrm { M } _ { n - 2 } \dots \mathrm { . . . } \mathrm { M } _ { 1 } ) ^ { - 1 } = \mathrm { M } _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \dots \mathrm { M } _ { n - 1 } ^ { - 1 }$ , it follows that

$$
\mathrm {A} = \mathrm {L U}.
$$

We notice that, due to (3.37), the subdiagonal entries of $\mathrm { L }$ are the multipliers $m _ { i k }$ produced by GEM, while the diagonal entries are equal to one.

Once the matrices L and U have been computed, solving the linear system consists only of solving successively the two triangular systems

$$
\mathrm {L y} = \mathbf {b}
$$

$$
\mathrm {U} \mathbf {x} = \mathbf {y}.
$$

The computational cost of the factorization process is obviously the same as that required by GEM.

The following result establishes a link between the leading dominant minors of a matrix and its LU factorization induced by GEM.

Theorem 3.4 Let A $\mathbf { \Psi } _ { \cdot } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n \times n }$ . The LU factorization of A with $l _ { i i } = 1$ for $i = 1 , \ldots , n$ exists and is unique iff the principal submatrices A $\textit { \textbf { \ i } }$ of A of order $i = 1 , \ldots , n - 1$ are nonsingular.

Proof. The existence of the LU factorization can be proved following the steps of the GEM. Here we prefer to pursue an alternative approach, which allows for proving at the same time both existence and uniqueness and that will be used again in later sections.

Let us assume that the leading minors A $\tau$ of A are nonsingular for $i = 1 , \dots , n -$ 1 and prove, by induction on $_ i$ , that under this hypothesis the LU factorization of $\mathrm { A } ( = \mathrm { A } _ { n } )$ with $l _ { i i } = 1$ for $i = 1 , \ldots , n$ , exists and is unique.

The property is obviouslyunique LU factorization of A e if of $i = 1$ . Arm therewith $_ { i - 1 }$ $\mathrm { A } _ { i - 1 } = \mathrm { L } ^ { ( i - 1 ) } \mathrm { U } ^ { ( i - 1 ) }$ $l _ { k k } ^ { ( i - 1 ) } = 1$ for $k = 1 , \dots , i - 1$ , and show that there exists an unique factorization also for A $_ { \tau }$ . We partition A $^ { - 2 }$ by block matrices as

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {i} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \mathrm {A} _ {i - 1} & \mathbf {c} \\ \mathbf {d} ^ {T} & a _ {i i} \end{array} \right]
$$

and look for a factorization of A $\boldsymbol { \cdot } \boldsymbol { \tau }$ of the form

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {i} = \mathrm {L} ^ {(i)} \mathrm {U} ^ {(i)} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \mathrm {L} ^ {(i - 1)} & \mathbf {0} \\ \mathbf {1} ^ {T} & 1 \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \mathrm {U} ^ {(i - 1)} & \mathbf {u} \\ \mathbf {0} ^ {T} & u _ {i i} \end{array} \right], \tag {3.38}
$$

having also partitioned by blocks the factors $\mathrm { L } ^ { ( i ) }$ and U $^ { ( i ) }$ . Computing the product of these two factors and equating by blocks the elements of A that the vectors l and $\mathbf { u }$ are the solutions to the linear systems $_ { \tau }$ ${ { \mathrm { L } } ^ { ( i - 1 ) } } { \bf { u } } = { \bf { c } }$ , it turns out , $\mathbf { l } ^ { T } \mathbf { U } ^ { ( i - 1 ) } = \mathbf { d } ^ { T }$ .

On the other hand, since $0 \neq \mathrm { d e t } ( \mathrm { A } _ { i - 1 } ) = \mathrm { d e t } ( \mathrm { L } ^ { ( i - 1 ) } ) \mathrm { d e t } ( \mathrm { U } ^ { ( i - 1 ) } )$ , the matrices $\mathrm { L } ^ { ( i - 1 ) }$ and $\mathrm { U } ^ { ( i - 1 ) }$ are nonsingular and, as a result, $\mathbf { u }$ and l exist and are unique.

Thus, there exists a unique factorization of $\mathrm { A } _ { i }$ , where $u _ { i i }$ is the unique solution of the equation $\mathrm { u } _ { i i } = \mathrm { a } _ { i i } - \mathrm { l } ^ { T } \mathrm { \mathbf { u } }$ . This completes the induction step of the proof.

It now remains to prove that, if the factorization at hand exists and is unique, then the first $n - 1$ leading minors of A must be nonsingular. We shall distinguish the case where A is singular and when it is nonsingular.

Let us start from the second one and assume that the LU factorization of A with $l _ { i i } = 1$ for $i = 1 , \ldots , n$ , exists and is unique. Then, due to (3.38), we have $\mathrm { A } _ { i } = \mathrm { L } ^ { \left( i \right) } \mathrm { U } ^ { \left( i \right) }$ for $i = 1 , \ldots , n$ . Thus

$$
\det  \left(\mathrm {A} _ {i}\right) = \det  \left(\mathrm {L} ^ {(i)}\right) \det  \left(\mathrm {U} ^ {(i)}\right) = \det  \left(\mathrm {U} ^ {(i)}\right) = u _ {1 1} u _ {2 2} \dots u _ {i i}, \tag {3.39}
$$

from which, taking $i = n$ and A nonsingular, we obtain $u _ { 1 1 } u _ { 2 2 } \ldots u _ { n n } \neq 0$ , and thus, necessarily, $\operatorname* { d e t } ( \mathrm { A } _ { i } ) = u _ { 1 1 } u _ { 2 2 } \dots u _ { i i } \neq 0$ for $i = 1 , \ldots , n - 1$ .

Now let A be a singular matrix and assume that (at least) one diagonal entry of U is equal to zero. Denote by $u _ { k k }$ the null entry of U with minimum index $k$ . Thanks to (3.38), the factorization can be computed without troubles until the $k + 1$ -th step. From that step on, since the matrix U $( k )$ is singular, existence and uniqueness of the vector $1 ^ { T }$ are certainly lost, and, thus, the same holds for the uniqueness of the factorization. In order for this not to occur before the process has factorized the whole matrix A, the $u _ { k k }$ entries must all be nonzero up to the index $k = n - 1$ included, and thus, due to (3.39), all the leading minors A $\boldsymbol { k }$ must be nonsingular for $k = 1 , \ldots , n - 1$ . ✸

From the above theorem we conclude that, if an $\mathrm { A } _ { i }$ , with $i = 1 , \ldots , n - 1$ , is singular, then the factorization may either not exist or not be unique.

Example 3.3 Consider the matrices

$$
\mathrm {B} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array} \right], \quad \mathrm {C} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array} \right], \quad \mathrm {D} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{array} \right].
$$

According to Theorem 3.4, the singular matrix B, having nonsingular leading minor $\mathrm { B _ { 1 } } = 1$ , admits a unique LU factorization. The remaining two examples outline that, if the assumptions of the theorem are not fulfilled, the factorization may fail to exist or be unique.

Actually, the nonsingular matrix C, with C1 singular, does not admit any factorization, while the (singular) matrix D, with D $\perp$ singular, admits an infinite number of factorizations of the form $\mathrm { D } = \mathrm { L } _ { \beta } \mathrm { U } _ { \beta }$ , with

$$
\mathrm {L} _ {\beta} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} 1 & 0 \\ \beta & 1 \end{array} \right], \quad \mathrm {U} _ {\beta} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 - \beta \end{array} \right], \quad \forall \beta \in \mathbb {R}.
$$

In the case where the LU factorization is unique, we point out that, because $\operatorname* { d e t } ( \mathrm { A } ) = \operatorname* { d e t } ( \mathrm { L U } ) = \operatorname* { d e t } ( \mathrm { L } ) \operatorname* { d e t } ( \mathrm { U } ) = \operatorname* { d e t } ( \mathrm { U } )$ , the determinant of A is given

by

$$
\det  (\mathrm {A}) = u _ {1 1} \dots u _ {n n}.
$$

Let us now recall the following property (referring for its proof to [GL89] or [Hig96]).

Property 3.2 If A is a matrix diagonally dominant by rows or by columns, then the LU factorization of A exists. In particular, if A is diagonally dominant by columns, then $| l _ { i j } | \le 1 \ \forall i , j$ .

In the proof of Theorem 3.4 we exploited the fact the the diagonal entries of L are equal to 1. In a similar manner, we could have fixed to 1 the diagonal entries of the upper triangular matrix U, obtaining a variant of GEM that will be considered in Section 3.3.4.

The freedom in setting up either the diagonal entries of L or those of U, implies that several LU factorizations exist which can be obtained one from the other by multiplication with a suitable diagonal matrix (see Section 3.4.1).

# 3.3.2 The Effect of Rounding Errors

If rounding errors are taken into account, the factorization process induced by GEM yields two matrices, $\widehat { \mathrm { L } }$ and $\hat { \mathrm { U } }$ , such that $\widehat { \mathrm { L U } } = \mathrm { A } + \delta \mathrm { A }$ , δA being a perturbation matrix. The size of such a perturbation can be estimated by

$$
| \delta A | \leq \frac {n u}{1 - n u} | \widehat {L} | | \widehat {U} |, \tag {3.40}
$$

where u is the roundoff unit (for the proof of this result we refer to [Hig89]). From (3.40) it is seen that the presence of small pivotal entries can make the right side of the inequality virtually unbounded, with a consequent loss of control on the size of the perturbation matrix $\delta$ A. The interest is thus in finding out estimates like (3.40) of the form

$$
| \delta A | \leq g (u) | A |,
$$

where $g ( \mathfrak { u } )$ is a suitable function of u. For instance, assuming that $\widehat { \mathrm { L } }$ and $\hat { \mathrm { U } }$ have nonnegative entries, then since $| \widehat { \mathrm { L } } | \ | \widehat { \mathrm { U } } | = | \widehat { \mathrm { L } } \widehat { \mathrm { U } } |$ one gets

$$
\left| \widehat {\mathrm {L}} \right| \left| \widehat {\mathrm {U}} \right| = \left| \widehat {\mathrm {L}} \widehat {\mathrm {U}} \right| = | \mathrm {A} + \delta \mathrm {A} | \leq | \mathrm {A} | + | \delta \mathrm {A} | \leq | \mathrm {A} | + \frac {n \mathrm {u}}{1 - n \mathrm {u}} | \widehat {\mathrm {L}} | | \widehat {\mathrm {U}} |, \tag {3.41}
$$

from which the desired bound is achieved by taking $g ( \mathbf { u } ) = n \mathbf { u } / ( 1 - 2 n \mathbf { u } )$ .

The technique of pivoting, examined in Section 3.5, keeps the size of the pivotal entries under control and makes it possible to obtain estimates like (3.41) for any matrix.

# 3.3.3 Implementation of LU Factorization

Since L is a lower triangular matrix with diagonal entries equal to 1 and U is upper triangular, it is possible (and convenient) to store the LU factorization directly in the same memory area that is occupied by the matrix A. More precisely, U is stored in the upper triangular part of A (including the diagonal), whilst L occupies the lower triangular portion of A (the diagonal entries of L are not stored since they are implicitly assumed to be 1).

A coding of the algorithm is reported in Program 4. The output matrix A contains the overwritten LU factorization.

Program 4 - lu kji : LU factorization of matrix A. kji version

function $[\mathsf{A}] = \mathsf{lu\_kji}$ (A) $[n,n] = \text{size(A)}$ for $k = 1:n - 1$ $\mathsf{A}(k + 1:n,k) = \mathsf{A}(k + 1:n,k) / \mathsf{A}(k,k)$ for $j = k + 1:n$ for $i = k + 1:n$ $\mathsf{A}(i,j) = \mathsf{A}(i,j) - \mathsf{A}(i,k)^{*}\mathsf{A}(k,j)$ end, end  
end

This implementation of the factorization algorithm is commonly referred to as the $k j i$ version, due to the order in which the cycles are executed. In a more appropriate notation, it is called the $S A X P Y - k j i$ version, due to the fact that the basic operation of the algorithm, which consists of multiplying a scalar $A$ by a vector $\mathbf { X }$ , summing another vector $\mathbf { Y }$ and then storing the result, is usually called SAXPY (i.e. $S$ calar A $X$ $P$ lus $Y$ ).

The factorization can of course be executed by following a different order. In general, the forms in which the cycle on index $i$ precedes the cycle on $j$ are called row-oriented, whilst the others are called column-oriented. As usual, this terminology refers to the fact that the matrix is accessed by rows or by columns.

An example of LU factorization, $j k i$ version and column-oriented, is given in Program 5. This version is commonly called $G A X P Y - j k i$ , since the basic operation (a product matrix-vector), is called GAXPY which stands for Generalized sAXPY (see for further details [DGK84]). In the GAXPY operation the scalar $A$ of the SAXPY operation is replaced by a matrix.

Program 5 - lu jki : LU factorization of matrix A. jki version

function $[\mathsf{A}] = \mathsf{lu\_jki}$ (A) $[n,n] = \mathsf{size(A)}$ for $j = 1:n$ for $k = 1:j - 1$ , for $i = k + 1:n$ $\mathsf{A}(i,j) = \mathsf{A}(i,j) - \mathsf{A}(i,k)^{*}\mathsf{A}(k,j)$ ;  
end, end  
for $i = j + 1:n$ , $\mathsf{A}(i,j) = \mathsf{A}(i,j) / \mathsf{A}(j,j)$ ; end  
end

# 3.3.4 Compact Forms of Factorization

Remarkable variants of LU factorization are the Crout factorization and Doolittle factorization, and are known also as compact forms of the Gauss elimination method. This name is due to the fact that these approaches require less intermediate results than the standard GEM to generate the factorization of A.

Computing the LU factorization of A is formally equivalent to solving the following nonlinear system of $n ^ { 2 }$ equations

$$
a _ {i j} = \sum_ {r = 1} ^ {\min  (i, j)} l _ {i r} u _ {r j}, \tag {3.42}
$$

the unknowns being the $n ^ { 2 } + n$ coefficients of the triangular matrices L and U. If we arbitrarily set $_ { n }$ coefficients to 1, for example the diagonal entries of L or U, we end up with the Doolittle and Crout methods, respectively, which provide an efficient way to solve system (3.42).

In fact, supposing that the first $k - 1$ columns of L and U are available and setting $l _ { k k } = 1$ (Doolittle method), the following equations are obtained from (3.42)

$$
\begin{array}{l} a_{kj} = \sum_{\substack{r = 1\\ k - 1}}^{k - 1}l_{kr}u_{rj} + \boxed{u_{kj}},\qquad j = k,\ldots ,n \\ a _ {i k} = \sum_ {r = 1} l _ {i r} u _ {r k} + \boxed {l _ {i k}} u _ {k k}, \quad i = k + 1, \dots , n. \\ \end{array}
$$

Note that these equations can be solved in a sequential way with respect to the boxed variables $u _ { k j }$ and $\boldsymbol { l } _ { i k }$ . From the Doolittle compact method we thus obtain first the $k$ -th row of U and then the $k$ -th column of L, as follows: for $k = 1 , \ldots , n$

$$
\begin{array}{l} u _ {k j} = a _ {k j} - \sum_ {r = 1} ^ {k - 1} l _ {k r} u _ {r j} \quad j = k, \dots , n \\ l _ {i k} = \frac {1}{u _ {k k}} \left(a _ {i k} - \sum_ {r = 1} ^ {k - 1} l _ {i r} u _ {r k}\right) \quad i = k + 1, \dots , n. \tag {3.43} \\ \end{array}
$$

The Crout factorization is generated similarly, computing first the $k$ -th column of L and then the $k$ -th row of U: for $k = 1 , \ldots , n$

$$
\begin{array}{l} l _ {i k} = a _ {i k} - \sum_ {r = 1} ^ {k - 1} l _ {i r} u _ {r k} \quad i = k, \dots , n \\ u _ {k j} = \frac {1}{l _ {k k}} \left(a _ {k j} - \sum_ {r = 1} ^ {k - 1} l _ {k r} u _ {r j}\right) \quad j = k + 1, \dots , n, \\ \end{array}
$$

where we set $u _ { k k } = 1$ . Recalling the notations introduced above, the Doolittle factorization is nothing but the $i j k$ version of GEM.

We provide in Program 6 the implementation of the Doolittle scheme. Notice that now the main computation is a dot product, so this scheme is also known as the $D O T - i j k$ version of GEM.

Program 6 - lu ijk : LU factorization of the matrix A: $i j k$ version

function $[\mathsf{A}] = \mathsf{lu\_ijk}$ (A) $\mathrm{[n,n] = size(A)}$ for $i = 1:n$ for $j = 2:i$ $\mathrm{A(i,j - 1) = A(i,j - 1) / A(j - 1,j - 1)}$ for $k = 1:j - 1$ $\mathrm{A(i,j) = A(i,j) - A(i,k)^{*}A(k,j)}$ end end for $j = i + 1:n$ for $k = 1:i - 1$ $\mathrm{A(i,j) = A(i,j) - A(i,k)^{*}A(k,j)}$ end end

# 3.4 Other Types of Factorization

We now address factorizations suitable for symmetric and rectangular matrices.

# 3.4.1 $L D M ^ { I }$ Factorization

It is possible to devise other types of factorizations of A removing the hypothesis that the elements of L are equal to one. Specifically, we will address some variants where the factorization of A is of the form

$$
\mathrm {A} = \mathrm {L D M} ^ {T}.
$$

where L, M $_ T$ and D are lower triangular, upper triangular and diagonal matrices, respectively.

After the construction of this factorization, the resolution of the system can be carried out solving first the lower triangular system Ly=b, then the diagonal one Dz=y, and finally the upper triangular system $\mathbf { M } ^ { T } \mathbf { x } { = } \mathbf { z }$ , with a cost of $n ^ { 2 } + n$ flops. In the symmetric case, we obtain M = L and the LDL $T '$ factorization can be computed with half the cost (see Section 3.4.2).

The LDL $_ T$ factorization enjoys a property analogous to the one in Theorem 3.4 for the LU factorization. In particular, the following result holds.

Theorem 3.5 If all the principal minors of a matrix A $\in \mathbb { R } ^ { n \times n }$ are nonzero then there exist a unique diagonal matrix D, a unique unit lower triangular matrix L and a unique unit upper triangular matrix M $T '$ , such that $\mathrm { A } = \mathrm { L D M } ^ { T }$ .

Proof. By Theorem 3.4 we already know that there exists a unique LU factorization of A with $l _ { i i } = 1$ for $i = 1 , \ldots , n$ . If we set the diagonal entries of D equal to $u _ { i i }$ (nonzero because U is nonsingular), then A = LU = LD(D $^ { - 1 }$ U). Upon defining $\mathrm { M } ^ { T } = \mathrm { D } ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { U }$ , the existence of the LDM $_ T$ factorization follows, where D−1U is a unit upper triangular matrix. The uniqueness of the LDM $_ T$ factorization is a consequence of the uniqueness of the LU factorization. $\diamond$

The above proof shows that, since the diagonal entries of D coincide with those of U, we could compute L, M $_ T$ and D starting from the LU factorization of A. It suffices to compute M $_ T$ as D $^ { - 1 }$ U. Nevertheless, this algorithm has the same cost as the standard LU factorization. Likewise, it is also possible to compute the three matrices of the factorization by enforcing the identity A=LDM $_ T$ entry by entry.

# 3.4.2 Symmetric and Positive Definite Matrices: The Cholesky Factorization

As already pointed out, the factorization LDM $T '$ simplifies considerably when A is symmetric because in such a case M=L, yielding the so-called LDM $T '$ factorization. The computational cost halves, with respect to the LU factorization, to about ( $m ^ { 3 } / 3$ ) flops.

As an example, the Hilbert matrix of order 3 admits the following LDL $T '$ factorization

$$
\mathrm {H} _ {3} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} 1 & \frac {1}{2} & \frac {1}{3} \\ \frac {1}{2} & \frac {1}{3} & \frac {1}{4} \\ \frac {1}{3} & \frac {1}{4} & \frac {1}{5} \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ \frac {1}{2} & 1 & 0 \\ \frac {1}{3} & 1 & 1 \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac {1}{1 2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac {1}{1 8 0} \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c c c} 1 & \frac {1}{2} & \frac {1}{3} \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array} \right].
$$

In the case that A is also positive definite, the diagonal entries of D in the LDL $_ T$ factorization are positive. Moreover, we have the following result.

Theorem 3.6 Let $\ b { \mathrm { A } } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { n \times n }$ be a symmetric and positive definite matrix. Then, there exists a unique upper triangular matrix H with positive diagonal entries such that

$$
\mathrm {A} = \mathrm {H} ^ {\mathrm {T}} \mathrm {H}. \tag {3.44}
$$

This factorization is called Cholesky factorization and the entries $h _ { i j }$ of $\mathrm { H } ^ { T }$ can be computed as follows: $h _ { 1 1 } = \sqrt { a _ { 1 1 } }$ and, for $i = 2 , \ldots , n$ ,

$$
\begin{array}{l} h _ {i j} = \left(a _ {i j} - \sum_ {k = 1} ^ {j - 1} h _ {i k} h _ {j k}\right) / h _ {j j}, \quad j = 1, \dots , i - 1, \\ h _ {i i} = \left(a _ {i i} - \sum_ {k = 1} ^ {i - 1} h _ {i k} ^ {2}\right) ^ {1 / 2}. \tag {3.45} \\ \end{array}
$$

Proof. Let us prove the theorem proceeding by induction on the size $_ i$ of the matrix (as done in Theorem 3.4), recalling that if $\mathrm { A } _ { i } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { i \times i }$ is symmetric positive definite, then all its principal submatrices enjoy the same property.

For $i = 1$ the result is obviously true. Thus, suppose that it holds for $i - 1$ and prove that it also holds for $_ i$ . There exists an upper triangular matrix $\mathrm H _ { i - 1 }$ such that $\mathrm { A } _ { i - 1 } = \mathrm { H } _ { i - 1 } ^ { T } \mathrm { H } _ { i - 1 }$ . Let us partition A $_ { \tau }$ as

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {i} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \mathrm {A} _ {i - 1} & \mathbf {v} \\ \mathbf {v} ^ {T} & \alpha \end{array} \right],
$$

with $\alpha \in \mathbb { R } ^ { + }$ , $\mathbf { v } ^ { T } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { i - 1 }$ and look for a factorization of A $\tau$ of the form

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {i} = \mathrm {H} _ {i} ^ {T} \mathrm {H} _ {i} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \mathrm {H} _ {i - 1} ^ {T} & \mathbf {0} \\ \mathbf {h} ^ {T} & \beta \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \mathrm {H} _ {i - 1} & \mathbf {h} \\ \mathbf {0} ^ {T} & \beta \end{array} \right].
$$

Enforcing the equality with the entries of A $_ { \tau }$ yields the equations $\mathrm { H } _ { i - 1 } ^ { T } \mathbf { h } = \textbf { v }$ and $\mathbf { h } ^ { T } \mathbf { h } + \boldsymbol { \beta } ^ { 2 } = \boldsymbol { \alpha }$ . The vector $\mathbf { h }$ is thus uniquely determined, since $\mathrm { H } _ { i - 1 } ^ { T }$ is nonsingular. As for $\beta$ , due to the properties of determinants

$$
0 <   \det (\mathrm {A} _ {i}) = \det (\mathrm {H} _ {i} ^ {T}) \det (\mathrm {H} _ {i}) = \beta^ {2} (\det (\mathrm {H} _ {i - 1})) ^ {2},
$$

we can conclude that it must be a real number. As a result, $\beta = \sqrt { \alpha - \mathbf { h } ^ { T } \mathbf { h } }$ is the desired diagonal entry and this concludes the inductive argument.

Let us now prove formulae (3.45). The fact that $h _ { 1 1 } = \sqrt { a _ { 1 1 } }$ is an immediate consequence of the induction argument for $i ~ = ~ 1$ . In the case of a generic $_ i$ , relations $( 3 . 4 5 ) _ { 1 }$ are the forward substitution formulae for the solution of the linear system $\mathrm { H } _ { i - 1 } ^ { T } \mathbf { h } = \mathbf { v } = ( a _ { 1 i } , a _ { 2 i } , \ldots , a _ { i - 1 , i } ) ^ { T }$ , while formulae (3.45)2 state that $\beta = \sqrt { \alpha - \mathbf { h } ^ { T } \mathbf { h } }$ , where $\alpha = a _ { i i }$ . ✸

The algorithm which implements (3.45) requires about ( $n ^ { 3 } / 3 )$ ) flops and it turns out to be stable with respect to the propagation of rounding errors. It can indeed be shown that the upper triangular matrix $\ddot { \mathrm { H } }$ is such that $\tilde { \mathrm { H } } ^ { T } \tilde { \mathrm { H } } = \mathrm { A } + \delta \mathrm { A }$ , where $\delta \mathrm { A }$ is a pertubation matrix such that $\| \delta \mathrm { A } \| _ { 2 } ~ \leq$ $8 n ( n + 1 ) { \tt u } \| { \tt A } \| _ { 2 }$ , when the rounding errors are considered and assuming that $2 n ( n + 1 ) \mathrm { u } \leq 1 - ( n + 1 ) \mathrm { u }$ (see [Wil68]).

Also, for the Cholesky factorization it is possible to overwrite the matrix $\mathrm { H } ^ { T }$ in the lower triangular portion of A, without any further memory storage. By doing so, both A and the factorization are preserved, noting that A is stored in the upper triangular section since it is symmetric and that its diagonal entries can be computed as $a _ { 1 1 } = h _ { 1 1 } ^ { 2 }$ , $\begin{array} { r } { a _ { i i } = h _ { i i } ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \imath - 1 } h _ { i k } ^ { 2 } } \end{array}$ , $i = 2 , \ldots , n$ .

An example of implementation of the Cholesky factorization is coded in Program 7.

Program 7 - chol2 : Cholesky factorization

function [A] = chol2 (A)

$$
[ n, n ] = \operatorname {s i z e} (A);
$$

for $k = 1:n - 1$ $\mathrm{A(k,k) = sqrt(A(k,k))}$ . $\mathrm{A(k + 1:n,k) = A(k + 1:n,k) / A(k,k)}$ for $j = k + 1:n$ $\mathrm{A(j:n,j) = A(j:n,j) - A(j:n,k)*A(j,k)}$ end   
end $\mathrm{A(n,n) = sqrt(A(n,n))}$

# 3.4.3 Rectangular Matrices: The QR Factorization

Definition 3.1 A matrix $\mathrm { ~ A ~ } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { m \times n }$ , with $m \ \geq \ n$ , admits a QR factorization if there exist an orthogonal matrix ${ \mathrm { Q } } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { m \times m }$ and an upper trapezoidal matrix $\mathrm { R } \in \mathbb { R } ^ { m \times n }$ with null rows from the $n + 1$ -th one on, such that

$$
\mathrm {A} = \mathrm {Q R}. \tag {3.46}
$$

This factorization can be constructed either using suitable transformation matrices (Givens or Householder matrices, see Section 5.6.1) or using the Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization algorithm discussed below.

It is also possible to generate a reduced version of the QR factorization (3.46), as stated in the following result.

Property 3.3 Let A $\in \mathbb { R } ^ { m \times n }$ be a matrix of rank n for which a QR factorization is known. Then there exists a unique factorization of A of the form

$$
\mathrm {A} = \widetilde {\mathrm {Q}} \widetilde {\mathrm {R}} \tag {3.47}
$$

where $\bar { \mathrm { \sf Q } }$ and $\widetilde { \mathrm { R } }$ are submatrices of Q and R given respectively by

$$
\widetilde {\mathrm {Q}} = \mathrm {Q} (1: m, 1: n), \quad \widetilde {\mathrm {R}} = \mathrm {R} (1: n, 1: n). \tag {3.48}
$$

Moreover, $\widetilde { \mathrm { \mathrm { Q } } }$ has orthonormal vector columns and $\widetilde { \mathrm { R } }$ is upper triangular and coincides with the Cholesky factor $\mathrm { H }$ of the symmetric positive definite matrix AT A, that is, $\mathrm { A } ^ { T } \mathrm { A } = \widetilde { \mathrm { R } } ^ { T } \widetilde { \mathrm { R } }$ .

If A has rank $n$ (i.e., full rank), then the column vectors of $\dot { \mathrm { ~ Q ~ } }$ form an orthonormal basis for the vector space range(A) (defined in (1.5)). As a consequence, constructing the QR factorization can also be interpreted as a procedure for generating an orthonormal basis for a given set of vectors. If A has rank $r \ < \ n$ , the QR factorization does not necessarily yield an orthonormal basis for range(A). However, one can obtain a factorization of the form

$$
\mathrm {Q} ^ {T} \mathrm {A P} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \mathrm {R} _ {1 1} & \mathrm {R} _ {1 2} \\ 0 & 0 \end{array} \right],
$$

![](images/3e765c73235347c7840656265729c2ce6b9d80d0caaf2aa600bc5a672709dec9.jpg)  
FIGURE 3.1. The reduced factorization. The matrices of the QR factorization are drawn in dashed lines

where Q is orthogonal, P is a permutation matrix and $\mathrm { R } _ { 1 1 }$ is a nonsingular upper triangular matrix of order $r$ .

In general, when using the QR factorization, we shall always refer to its reduced form (3.47) as it finds a remarkable application in the solution of overdetermined systems (see Section 3.13).

The matrix factors $\dot { \mathrm { ~ Q ~ } }$ and $\bar { \mathrm { ~ R ~ } }$ in (3.47) can be computed using the Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization. Starting from a set of linearly independent vectors, $\mathbf { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathbf { x } _ { n }$ , this algorithm generates a new set of mutually orthogonal vectors, ${ \bf q } _ { 1 } , \ldots , { \bf q } _ { n }$ , given by

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {q} _ {1} = \mathbf {x} _ {1}, \\ \mathbf {q} _ {k + 1} = \mathbf {x} _ {k + 1} - \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {k} \frac {\left(\mathbf {q} _ {i} , \mathbf {x} _ {k + 1}\right)}{\left(\mathbf {q} _ {i} , \mathbf {q} _ {i}\right)} \mathbf {q} _ {i}, \quad k = 1, \dots , n - 1. \tag {3.49} \\ \end{array}
$$

Denoting by $\mathbf { a } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mathbf { a } _ { n }$ the column vectors of A, we set $\tilde { \mathbf { q } } _ { 1 } = \mathbf { a } _ { 1 } / \lVert \mathbf { a } _ { 1 } \rVert _ { 2 }$ and, for $k = 1 , \ldots , n - 1$ , compute the column vectors of $\dot { \mathrm { ~ Q ~ } }$ as

$$
\tilde {\mathbf {q}} _ {k + 1} = \mathbf {q} _ {k + 1} / \| \mathbf {q} _ {k + 1} \| _ {2},
$$

where

$$
\mathbf {q} _ {k + 1} = \mathbf {a} _ {k + 1} - \sum_ {j = 1} ^ {k} (\tilde {\mathbf {q}} _ {j}, \mathbf {a} _ {k + 1}) \tilde {\mathbf {q}} _ {j}.
$$

Next, imposing that A=Q˜ R and exploiting the fact that ˜ $\dot { \mathrm { ~ Q ~ } }$ is orthogonal (that is, $\tilde { \mathrm { Q } } ^ { - 1 } = \tilde { \mathrm { Q } } ^ { T }$ ), the entries of $\tilde { \mathrm { R } }$ can easily be computed. The overall computational cost of the algorithm is of the order of $m n ^ { 2 }$ flops.

It is also worth noting that if A has full rank, the matrix $\mathrm { A } ^ { T } \mathrm { A }$ is symmetric and positive definite (see Section 1.9) and thus it admits a unique Cholesky factorization of the form $\mathrm { H } ^ { T } \mathrm { H }$ . On the other hand, since the orthogonality of $\bar { \mathrm { Q } }$ implies

$$
\mathrm {H} ^ {T} \mathrm {H} = \mathrm {A} ^ {T} \mathrm {A} = \tilde {\mathrm {R}} ^ {T} \tilde {\mathrm {Q}} ^ {T} \tilde {\mathrm {Q}} \tilde {\mathrm {R}} = \tilde {\mathrm {R}} ^ {T} \tilde {\mathrm {R}},
$$

we conclude that $\bar { \mathrm { ~ R ~ } }$ is actually the Cholesky factor H of $\mathrm { A } ^ { T } \mathrm { A }$ . Thus, the diagonal entries of $\ddot { \mathrm { ~ R ~ } }$ are all nonzero only if A has full rank.

The Gram-Schmidt method is of little practical use since the generated vectors lose their linear independence due to rounding errors.
